text: This paper introduces Stanford CoreNLP, an integrated suite of natural language processing tools designed as an extensible Java pipeline. The toolkit provides a comprehensive range of linguistic analysis capabilities, including tokenization, part-of-speech tagging, named entity recognition, syntactic parsing, and coreference resolution. Historically developed to replace fragmented, ad hoc processing scripts with a unified API, the system focuses on a lightweight architecture that represents annotations as typesafe maps. We examine the design philosophy behind CoreNLP, attributing its widespread adoption in both academia and industry to its "minimal baggage" approach: it offers high-quality, robust components with a straightforward interface that avoids the steep learning curves associated with heavier frameworks like UIMA or GATE. The paper details the system’s primary annotators, its support for multiple human languages, and the ease with which users can implement and integrate custom analysis modules. By emphasizing simplicity, documentation, and ease of deployment, Stanford CoreNLP serves as a highly accessible yet powerful solution for diverse text analytics tasks.
